title: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis

items.subtopic: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Diseases | BACKGROUND AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
items.title: Understanding Familial Mediterranean Fever (FMF) Pathogenesis
items.content: FMF is an inherited autoinflammatory disease characterized by recurrent fever episodes with serosal, synovial, or cutaneous inflammation. The innate immune system plays a key role in its pathogenesis through the activation of myeloid effector cells and germline receptors for pathogen-associated molecular patterns.
keyPoints: FMF is primarily an autoinflammatory disorder due to low levels of high-titer autoantibodies or antigen-specific T cells.
The FMF gene encodes pyrin, a protein involved in forming the inflammasome complex that activates caspase-1 and promotes IL-1β and IL-18 secretion.
FMF-associated mutations are gain-of-function, reducing the threshold for pyrin inflammasome activation.
Historical pandemics like bubonic plague may have selected for FMF founder mutations.
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Diseases | BACKGROUND AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Diseases | BACKGROUND AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
items.title: Genetic and Epidemiological Aspects of Familial Mediterranean Fever (FMF)
items.content: FMF is a recessively inherited disease, though some cases show dominant inheritance patterns. It affects specific populations with high carrier frequencies due to potential selective advantages.
keyPoints: FMF was first recognized among Armenians, Arabs, Turks, and non-Ashkenazi Jews but now identified in Ashkenazi Jews, Italians, and other Mediterranean/Middle Eastern groups.
Carrier frequency can be as high as 1:10 in affected populations.
A positive family history is found in ~50% of cases due to small family sizes.
Genetic testing has revealed FMF mutations with dominant inheritance patterns in rare instances.
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Diseases | BACKGROUND AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.subtopic: ACUTE ATTACKS
items.title: Characteristics of FMF Attacks: Timing and Symptoms
items.content: FMF attacks often begin in early infancy, with 90% experiencing the first attack by age 20. These episodes typically last 24–72 hours but can vary significantly in frequency and duration. Common triggers include physical exertion, emotional stress, or menses, while pregnancy may lead to remission.
keyPoints: FMF attacks often begin in early infancy; 90% have their first attack by age 20.
Typical episodes last 24–72 hours, but frequency and duration can vary widely.
Triggers include physical exertion, emotional stress, or menses; pregnancy may lead to remission.
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · ACUTE ATTACKS

items.subtopic: ACUTE ATTACKS
items.title: Abdominal and Pleural Manifestations of FMF
items.content: Over 90% of FMF patients experience abdominal attacks characterized by pain, distention, and tenderness. Severe cases may show absent bowel sounds and air-fluid levels on radiographs. Pleural attacks manifest as unilateral chest pain with possible atelectasis or effusion.
keyPoints: Over 90% of FMF patients have abdominal attacks; symptoms range from mild to severe.
Severe abdominal attacks can present with absent bowel sounds, rigidity, and air-fluid levels on radiographs.
Pleural attacks cause unilateral chest pain and may show atelectasis or effusion on radiographs.
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · ACUTE ATTACKS

items.subtopic: ACUTE ATTACKS
items.title: Arthritis and Cutaneous Manifestations in FMF
items.content: FMF arthritis is most common among M694V homozygotes, typically affecting one joint like the knee or ankle. Erysipelas-like erythema, a characteristic rash, occurs on the lower extremities and may accompany other symptoms.
keyPoints: FMF arthritis often affects mono-articular joints such as the knee, ankle, or hip.
Erysipelas-like erythema is a characteristic rash seen in FMF, especially in M694V homozygotes.
Arthritis and cutaneous manifestations may co-occur with abdominal pain, pleurisy, or arthritis.
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · ACUTE ATTACKS

items.subtopic: AMYLOIDOSIS | DIAGNOSIS
items.title: Recognizing Risk Factors for Amyloidosis in FMF Patients
items.content: Amyloidosis is a significant complication of Familial Mediterranean Fever (FMF), particularly before colchicine prophylaxis was common. It involves the deposition of serum amyloid A fragments and should be suspected in patients with proteinuria between attacks. Renal or rectal biopsy is typically used for diagnosis.
keyPoints: Amyloidosis risk factors include M694V homozygous genotype, positive family history (independent of FMF mutational status), SAA1 genotype, male gender, noncompliance with colchicine or IL-1 inhibitory therapy, and national origin related to infant mortality rates.
Proteinuria between attacks is a red flag for amyloidosis in FMF patients.
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · AMYLOIDOSIS | DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS
items.title: Diagnosing FMF: Clinical Judgment and Genetic Testing
items.content: For typical cases of Familial Mediterranean Fever, experienced physicians can often diagnose based on clinical grounds alone. However, genetic testing is valuable in ambiguous cases or for less experienced practitioners. Most severe disease-associated mutations are found in exon 10 of the gene.
keyPoints: Clinical criteria sets for FMF have high sensitivity and specificity where pretest probability is high.
~70% of patients with clinically typical FMF have two identifiable mutations in trans, indicating a gain-of-function mutation effect on inflammasome activation.
M694V homozygotes often present earlier with more severe symptoms like arthritis, rash, and amyloidosis.
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · AMYLOIDOSIS | DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Familial Mediterranean Fever
items.title: Colchicine as First-Line Treatment in FMF
items.content: Daily oral colchicine is the initial treatment of choice for Familial Mediterranean Fever (FMF), reducing attack frequency and preventing amyloidosis. Intermittent dosing at attack onset lacks proven efficacy in preventing amyloidosis.
keyPoints: Colchicine decreases attack frequency and intensity, prevents amyloidosis with daily prophylaxis.
Usual adult dose: 1.2–1.8 mg/d; effective symptom reduction in two-thirds of patients, some improvement in >90%.
Common side effects include bloating, abdominal cramps, lactose intolerance, diarrhea.
Colchicine should be continued during pregnancy due to miscarriage risk from FMF attacks outweighing potential fetal risks.
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · Familial Mediterranean Fever

items.subtopic: Familial Mediterranean Fever
items.title: Alternative Treatments for Colchicine-Resistant or Intolerant FMF Patients
items.content: For FMF patients unresponsive to colchicine, IL-1 inhibitors such as canakinumab, rilonacept, and anakinra are alternatives. Canakinumab is FDA-approved based on a phase 3 trial.
keyPoints: Canakinumab (monoclonal anti-IL-1 $ \beta $ antibody) approved for FMF; administered monthly subcutaneously.
Rilonacept, a recombinant IL-1 receptor fusion protein, reduces attack frequency in small trials.
Anakinra, an IL-1 receptor antagonist, used anecdotally to prevent acute attacks and reduce amyloid deposits.
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · Familial Mediterranean Fever

items.subtopic: TNF RECEPTOR-ASSOCIATED PERIODIC SYNDROME
items.title: Understanding TRAPS: Genetic Basis and Treatment Options
items.content: TRAPS is a dominantly inherited syndrome caused by mutations in the TNFR1 gene, leading to periodic inflammatory attacks. These episodes can last from days to weeks and often include symptoms like ocular inflammation and migratory myalgia. Diagnosis relies on identifying characteristic symptoms alongside a TNFRSF1A mutation. Treatment options include glucocorticoids and IL-1 inhibitors such as canakinumab, with etanercept being less favorable due to potential exacerbation of attacks.
keyPoints: Caused by dominantly inherited mutations in the 55-kDa TNF receptor (TNFR1, p55).
Episodes often begin in childhood and may last from 1-2 days up to several weeks.
~15% of untreated patients develop amyloidosis.
Diagnosis involves detecting a TNFRSF1A mutation with characteristic symptoms.
Common mutations include R92Q and P46L, which may act as functional polymorphisms.
Etanercept can exacerbate attacks; IL-1 inhibitors like canakinumab are beneficial.
Canakinumab has FDA approval for TRAPS treatment.
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · TNF RECEPTOR-ASSOCIATED PERIODIC SYNDROME | HYPERIMMUNOGLOBULINEMIA D WITH PERIODIC FEVER SYNDROME (ALSO KNOWN AS MEVALONATE KINASE DEFICIENCY)

items.subtopic: HYPERIMMUNOGLOBULINEMIA D WITH PERIODIC FEVER SYNDROME (ALSO KNOWN AS MEVALONATE KINASE DEFICIENCY)
items.title: Diagnosing and Treating HIDS/MKD: Genetic Insights and Therapeutic Approaches
items.content: HIDS, also known as MKD, is a recessively inherited syndrome primarily affecting individuals of northern European descent. It results from mutations in the MVK gene, impacting cholesterol synthesis pathways and leading to periodic fever attacks starting in infancy. Symptoms include cervical adenopathy, rash, and aphthous ulcers. Diagnosis involves elevated urinary mevalonate levels during attacks, with serum IgD not correlating with disease activity. Canakinumab is FDA-approved for treatment.
keyPoints: Recessively inherited syndrome caused by mutations in the MVK gene.
Affects individuals primarily of northern European ancestry.
Symptoms include painful cervical adenopathy and a maculopapular rash lasting 3-5 days.
Amyloidosis associated with specific genotypes like V377I/I268T.
Disease activity not related to serum IgD levels; urinary mevalonate is elevated during attacks.
Canakinumab has FDA approval for HIDS/MKD treatment.
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · TNF RECEPTOR-ASSOCIATED PERIODIC SYNDROME | HYPERIMMUNOGLOBULINEMIA D WITH PERIODIC FEVER SYNDROME (ALSO KNOWN AS MEVALONATE KINASE DEFICIENCY)

items.subtopic: NLRP3-ASSOCIATED AUTOINFLAMMATORY DISEASE (ALSO KNOWN AS THE CRYOPYRINOPATHIES OR CRYOPYRIN-ASSOCIATED PERIODIC SYNDROMES)
items.title: Recognizing and Treating Cryopyrinopathies
items.content: Cryopyrinopathies, including FCAS, MWS, and NOMID, are caused by mutations in NLRP3. These disorders manifest with symptoms like fever, rash, arthralgia, and hearing loss, varying in severity. They involve increased IL-1 $ \beta $ release due to inflammasome activation. Treatment includes IL-1 inhibitors such as canakinumab for FCAS and MWS, rilonacept for these conditions, and anakinra for NOMID.
keyPoints: Mutations in NLRP3 gene cause FCAS, MWS, and NOMID.
Symptoms: fever, rash, arthralgia; hearing loss in MWS; chronic meningitis in NOMID.
Increased IL-1 $ \beta $ release due to inflammasome activation.
IL-1 inhibitors are effective treatments: canakinumab (bimonthly) for FCAS and MWS, rilonacept (weekly), anakinra (daily) for NOMID.
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · NLRP3-ASSOCIATED AUTOINFLAMMATORY DISEASE (ALSO KNOWN AS THE CRYOPYRINOPATHIES OR CRYOPYRIN-ASSOCIATED PERIODIC SYNDROMES)

items.subtopic: NLRP3-ASSOCIATED AUTOINFLAMMATORY DISEASE (ALSO KNOWN AS THE CRYOPYRINOPATHIES OR CRYOPYRIN-ASSOCIATED PERIODIC SYNDROMES)
items.title: Understanding Somatic Mosaicism in NLRP3
items.content: Approximately one-third of NOMID patients without germline NLRP3 mutations exhibit somatic mosaicism. These patients also respond well to IL-1 inhibition, similar to those with germline mutations. Somatic mosaicism is rarely seen in Schnitzler's syndrome, which presents with fever, rash, elevated acute-phase reactants, and monoclonal IgM gammopathy.
keyPoints: One-third of NOMID patients without NLRP3 mutations have somatic mosaicism.
Somatic mosaicism responds to IL-1 inhibition.
Rare in Schnitzler's syndrome with symptoms like fever, urticarial rash, and monoclonal IgM gammopathy.
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · NLRP3-ASSOCIATED AUTOINFLAMMATORY DISEASE (ALSO KNOWN AS THE CRYOPYRINOPATHIES OR CRYOPYRIN-ASSOCIATED PERIODIC SYNDROMES)

items.subtopic: PERIODIC FEVER WITH APHTHOUS STOMATITIS, PHARYNGITIS, AND CERVICAL ADENITIS | OTHER INHERITED AUTOINFLAMMATORY DISEASES | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.title: PFAPA Syndrome: Clinical Features and Management Options
items.content: Periodic fever with aphthous stomatitis, pharyngitis, and cervical adenitis (PFAPA) is a common periodic fever syndrome in children characterized by regular episodes that often resolve by early adulthood. It has familial tendencies but does not follow Mendelian inheritance patterns.
keyPoints: Most common periodic fever syndrome in children
Episodes have almost clock-like regularity
Tends to resolve by early adulthood
Therapeutic options include intermittent glucocorticoids, daily oral colchicine, cimetidine, apremilast, or tonsillectomy/adenoidectomy
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · PERIODIC FEVER WITH APHTHOUS STOMATITIS, PHARYNGITIS, AND CERVICAL ADENITIS | OTHER INHERITED AUTOINFLAMMATORY DISEASES | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: PERIODIC FEVER WITH APHTHOUS STOMATITIS, PHARYNGITIS, AND CERVICAL ADENITIS | OTHER INHERITED AUTOINFLAMMATORY DISEASES | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.title: Inflammasomopathies: Genetic Basis and Clinical Presentation
items.content: Inflammasomopathies are caused by mutations in genes related to inflammasome components or their regulators. These include FMF, HIDS, CAPS, PAPA syndrome, and conditions linked to NLRC4 mutations.
keyPoints: Caused by germline or somatic mutations
Examples: FMF, HIDS, CAPS, PAPA syndrome
PAPA syndrome involves pyogenic arthritis, pyoderma gangrenosum, and acne
IL-1 inhibition is effective for arthritic manifestations in some cases
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · PERIODIC FEVER WITH APHTHOUS STOMATITIS, PHARYNGITIS, AND CERVICAL ADENITIS | OTHER INHERITED AUTOINFLAMMATORY DISEASES | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: PERIODIC FEVER WITH APHTHOUS STOMATITIS, PHARYNGITIS, AND CERVICAL ADENITIS | OTHER INHERITED AUTOINFLAMMATORY DISEASES | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.title: VEXAS Syndrome: Epidemiology and Clinical Features
items.content: VEXAS syndrome is a severe adult-onset autoinflammatory disease linked to somatic mutations in an ubiquitylation enzyme, presenting with symptoms like relapsing polychondritis and vasculitis.
keyPoints: Severe adult-onset autoinflammatory disease
Linked to myeloid-restricted somatic mutations
Estimated prevalence: ~1 in 4000 men over age 50
Clinical presentations include relapsing polychondritis, Sweet's syndrome, vasculitis

items.subtopic: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Diseases | BACKGROUND AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
items.question: Which of the following best describes the inheritance pattern of Familial Mediterranean Fever (FMF)?
options: Recessively inherited with occasional dominant cases.
items.explanation: FMF is generally regarded as recessively inherited, but there are clear-cut clinical cases with only a single genetic mutation and some rare mutations show dominant inheritance.
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Diseases | BACKGROUND AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Diseases | BACKGROUND AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
items.question: What is the role of pyrin in Familial Mediterranean Fever (FMF)?
options: Pyrin nucleates the formation of a macromolecular inflammasome to activate caspase-1.
Pyrin is involved in blocking leukocyte cytoskeletal assembly.
Pyrin prevents IL-1β and IL-18 secretion.
items.explanation: Pyrin nucleates the formation of a macromolecular pyrin inflammasome to activate caspase-1, leading to IL-1β and IL-18 secretion and gasdermin D-mediated cell death (pyroptosis).
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Diseases | BACKGROUND AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Diseases | BACKGROUND AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
items.question: Which populations have been documented to have increasing frequency of FMF with genetic testing?
options: Only Armenians, Arabs, Turks, and non-Ashkenazi Jews.
Ashkenazi Jews, Italians, and other Mediterranean and Middle Eastern populations.
Exclusively among North African and Iraqi Jews.
items.explanation: With genetic testing, FMF has been documented with increasing frequency among Ashkenazi Jews, Italians, and other Mediterranean and Middle Eastern populations.
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Diseases | BACKGROUND AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.subtopic: ACUTE ATTACKS
items.question: Which of the following is a common characteristic of FMF pleural attacks?
options: Bilateral chest pain with associated fever
Unilateral, sharp, stabbing chest pain with possible atelectasis and effusion on radiographs
Pleural thickening without any preceding symptoms
items.explanation: FMF pleural attacks are usually manifested by unilateral, sharp, stabbing chest pain, often accompanied by atelectasis and sometimes an effusion on radiographs.

items.explanation: Typical FMF episodes generally last 24–72 hours.

options: Erysipelas-like erythema on the dorsum of the foot, ankle, or lower leg
items.explanation: The most characteristic cutaneous manifestation of FMF is erysipelas-like erythema, typically occurring on the dorsum of the foot, ankle, or lower leg.

items.explanation: Side effects such as bloating, abdominal cramps, lactose intolerance, and diarrhea can be minimized by starting colchicine at a low dose.

It increases the risk of developing amyloidosis.
items.explanation: Intravenous colchicine should not be administered to patients already taking oral colchicine due to severe, sometimes fatal toxicity.

items.statement: The term autoinflammatory is used for FMF because of the presence of high-titer autoantibodies or antigen-specific T cells.
items.explanation: FMF and similar disorders are termed autoinflammatory due to the relative infrequency of high-titer autoantibodies or antigen-specific T cells.

items.statement: Pyrin mutations in FMF are considered loss-of-function.
items.explanation: FMF-associated mutations are considered gain-of-function, reducing the threshold for pyrin inflammasome activation.

items.subtopic: ACUTE ATTACKS
items.statement: FMF arthritis is most frequent among individuals homozygous for the M694V mutation.
items.explanation: Acute arthritis in FMF is most frequent among individuals homozygous for the M694V mutation.
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · ACUTE ATTACKS

items.subtopic: ACUTE ATTACKS
items.statement: FMF attacks are always associated with chronic radiographic changes.
items.explanation: Even after repeated arthritic attacks, radiographic changes in FMF are rare.
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · ACUTE ATTACKS

items.subtopic: AMYLOIDOSIS | DIAGNOSIS
items.statement: Genetic testing for FMF mutations is only useful when clinical criteria are inconclusive.
items.explanation: Genetic testing can be a useful adjunct even in cases where clinical criteria suggest FMF, especially for less experienced physicians.
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · AMYLOIDOSIS | DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: AMYLOIDOSIS | DIAGNOSIS
items.statement: The E148Q variant is more likely to cause clinical FMF than affect overall levels of inflammation.
items.explanation: The E148Q variant affects overall levels of inflammation rather than causing clinical FMF.
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · AMYLOIDOSIS | DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Familial Mediterranean Fever
items.statement: Colchicine is usually discontinued during pregnancy due to its effects on fetal development.
items.explanation: Colchicine is continued during pregnancy because the risk of miscarriage from FMF attacks outweighs potential effects on fetal development.
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · Familial Mediterranean Fever

items.subtopic: Familial Mediterranean Fever
items.statement: Cyclosporine can lead to colchicine toxicity in patients who have undergone renal transplantation for amyloidosis.
items.explanation: Cyclosporine inhibits hepatic excretion of colchicine, potentially leading to toxicity in such patients.
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · Familial Mediterranean Fever

items.subtopic: TNF RECEPTOR-ASSOCIATED PERIODIC SYNDROME | HYPERIMMUNOGLOBULINEMIA D WITH PERIODIC FEVER SYNDROME (ALSO KNOWN AS MEVALONATE KINASE DEFICIENCY)
items.statement: The diagnosis of TRAPS is based solely on clinical symptoms.
items.explanation: The diagnosis of TRAPS requires the demonstration of a TNFRSF1A mutation in addition to characteristic symptoms.
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · TNF RECEPTOR-ASSOCIATED PERIODIC SYNDROME | HYPERIMMUNOGLOBULINEMIA D WITH PERIODIC FEVER SYNDROME (ALSO KNOWN AS MEVALONATE KINASE DEFICIENCY)

items.subtopic: TNF RECEPTOR-ASSOCIATED PERIODIC SYNDROME | HYPERIMMUNOGLOBULINEMIA D WITH PERIODIC FEVER SYNDROME (ALSO KNOWN AS MEVALONATE KINASE DEFICIENCY)
items.statement: HIDS attacks usually begin in infancy and last 3–5 days.
items.explanation: The passage states that HIDS attacks typically start in infancy and have a duration of 3–5 days.
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · TNF RECEPTOR-ASSOCIATED PERIODIC SYNDROME | HYPERIMMUNOGLOBULINEMIA D WITH PERIODIC FEVER SYNDROME (ALSO KNOWN AS MEVALONATE KINASE DEFICIENCY)

items.subtopic: NLRP3-ASSOCIATED AUTOINFLAMMATORY DISEASE (ALSO KNOWN AS THE CRYOPYRINOPATHIES OR CRYOPYRIN-ASSOCIATED PERIODIC SYNDROMES)
items.statement: All patients with clinical manifestations of NOMID have germline mutations in NLRP3.
items.explanation: Approximately one-third of patients with NOMID do not have germline mutations but are mosaic for somatic NLRP3 mutations.
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · NLRP3-ASSOCIATED AUTOINFLAMMATORY DISEASE (ALSO KNOWN AS THE CRYOPYRINOPATHIES OR CRYOPYRIN-ASSOCIATED PERIODIC SYNDROMES)

items.subtopic: NLRP3-ASSOCIATED AUTOINFLAMMATORY DISEASE (ALSO KNOWN AS THE CRYOPYRINOPATHIES OR CRYOPYRIN-ASSOCIATED PERIODIC SYNDROMES)
items.statement: IL-1 inhibition is the treatment of choice for Schnitzler's syndrome.
items.explanation: Schnitzler's syndrome responds dramatically to IL-1 inhibition.
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · NLRP3-ASSOCIATED AUTOINFLAMMATORY DISEASE (ALSO KNOWN AS THE CRYOPYRINOPATHIES OR CRYOPYRIN-ASSOCIATED PERIODIC SYNDROMES)

items.statement: FMF is most frequently observed in Mediterranean and Middle Eastern populations.
items.explanation: The passage notes that FMF is most frequently observed in Mediterranean and Middle Eastern populations.
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · PERIODIC FEVER WITH APHTHOUS STOMATITIS, PHARYNGITIS, AND CERVICAL ADENITIS | OTHER INHERITED AUTOINFLAMMATORY DISEASES | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: PERIODIC FEVER WITH APHTHOUS STOMATITIS, PHARYNGITIS, AND CERVICAL ADENITIS | OTHER INHERITED AUTOINFLAMMATORY DISEASES | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.statement: HIDS is most frequently observed in Mediterranean and Middle Eastern populations.
items.explanation: The passage states that HIDS is particularly common in northern European populations, especially the Dutch.
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · PERIODIC FEVER WITH APHTHOUS STOMATITIS, PHARYNGITIS, AND CERVICAL ADENITIS | OTHER INHERITED AUTOINFLAMMATORY DISEASES | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Diseases | BACKGROUND AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
items.assertion: Certain bacterial toxins trigger pyrin inflammasome activation as part of normal host defense.
items.reason: These toxins block leukocyte cytoskeletal assembly by inactivating RhoA GTPase, leading to pyrin inflammasome activation.
items.explanation: The reason correctly explains the assertion that bacterial toxins trigger pyrin inflammasome activation as part of normal host defense.
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Diseases | BACKGROUND AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.subtopic: ACUTE ATTACKS
items.assertion: FMF patients often experience abdominal attacks.
items.reason: Over 90% of FMF patients experience abdominal attacks at some time, ranging in severity and sometimes showing fluid on CT scans.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is a correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · ACUTE ATTACKS

items.subtopic: ACUTE ATTACKS
items.assertion: FMF attacks can be triggered by physical exertion or emotional stress.
items.reason: Pregnancy is associated with increased frequency of FMF attacks.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false as pregnancy may be associated with remission, not an increase in attack frequency.
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · ACUTE ATTACKS

items.subtopic: TNF RECEPTOR-ASSOCIATED PERIODIC SYNDROME | HYPERIMMUNOGLOBULINEMIA D WITH PERIODIC FEVER SYNDROME (ALSO KNOWN AS MEVALONATE KINASE DEFICIENCY)
items.assertion: Monoclonal anti-TNF antibodies should be avoided in TRAPS treatment.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining why monoclonal anti-TNF antibodies should be avoided.
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · TNF RECEPTOR-ASSOCIATED PERIODIC SYNDROME | HYPERIMMUNOGLOBULINEMIA D WITH PERIODIC FEVER SYNDROME (ALSO KNOWN AS MEVALONATE KINASE DEFICIENCY)

items.subtopic: NLRP3-ASSOCIATED AUTOINFLAMMATORY DISEASE (ALSO KNOWN AS THE CRYOPYRINOPATHIES OR CRYOPYRIN-ASSOCIATED PERIODIC SYNDROMES)
items.assertion: Patients with Muckle-Wells syndrome develop sensorineural hearing loss over time.
items.reason: MWS is characterized by fevers, abdominal pain, limb pain, arthritis, conjunctivitis, and sensorineural hearing loss.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · NLRP3-ASSOCIATED AUTOINFLAMMATORY DISEASE (ALSO KNOWN AS THE CRYOPYRINOPATHIES OR CRYOPYRIN-ASSOCIATED PERIODIC SYNDROMES)

items.subtopic: PERIODIC FEVER WITH APHTHOUS STOMATITIS, PHARYNGITIS, AND CERVICAL ADENITIS | OTHER INHERITED AUTOINFLAMMATORY DISEASES | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.assertion: Blau's syndrome is characterized by granulomatous dermatitis, uveitis, and arthritis.
items.reason: Blau's syndrome is caused by gain-of-function mutations in NOD2/CARD15.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: Familial Mediterranean Fever and Other Hereditary Autoinflammatory Dis · PERIODIC FEVER WITH APHTHOUS STOMATITIS, PHARYNGITIS, AND CERVICAL ADENITIS | OTHER INHERITED AUTOINFLAMMATORY DISEASES | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: PERIODIC FEVER WITH APHTHOUS STOMATITIS, PHARYNGITIS, AND CERVICAL ADENITIS | OTHER INHERITED AUTOINFLAMMATORY DISEASES | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.assertion: ADA2 deficiency presents exclusively with vasculopathy.
items.reason: Recessive loss-of-function germline mutations in ADA2 cause a vasculopathy that can manifest as livedoid rash, early-onset lacunar strokes, and/or polyarteritis nodosa.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because ADA2 deficiency may also present with immunodeficiency or bone marrow failure.
